beats.title: THE CITADEL AWAKENS
beats.subtitle: Security & Privacy Controls · NIST SP 800-53r5
say: Every gap, every shortcut, every forgotten door — I will find it.
Not tonight. Welcome to the Aegis Citadel. I'm Vega, and I'll be your guide.
Every modern organization runs on information. Protect it, and you thrive. Lose it, and you fall.

beats.title: AEGIS CITADEL
say: Picture an organization's information as a walled city. At its heart sits the Crown Data — the records, secrets, and services everyone depends on.
I don't want your walls. I want what they guard — the Crown Data. Lose it once, and there is no second copy of trust.
I'm Nova — just started here, and honestly? I'm new to all of this. Can we start from the very beginning?
We will. First, a few words everyone uses and almost no one defines.

beats.expand: National Institute of Standards and Technology
beats.plain: A U.S. government lab that writes widely used security and technology guidance. It isn't a police force — but its playbooks are followed across government, and far beyond it.
say: Okay, so who writes all this stuff?
That would be NIST. Think of them as the standards lab for digital defense.

beats.plain: The people, hardware, software, networks, and data that work together to collect, store, process, or move information.
beats.example: A hospital's patient-records app, a company's email, a bank's website — even the phone in your pocket.
say: The thing we're protecting is an information system.
Far more. The people and processes count too.

beats.plain: A safeguard or countermeasure you put in place to protect a system and its information — and to bring risk down.
beats.example: A lock on a door, a password requirement, a smoke alarm, or a backup of your files.
say: A safeguard. Something deliberate that reduces risk. NIST SP 800-53 is a giant, organized catalog of them.

beats.plain: How likely something bad is, multiplied by how badly it would hurt. Security is the work of lowering risk to a level you can live with.
beats.example: An unlocked door (a vulnerability) plus a burglar nearby (a threat) makes a real risk of theft.
say: People keep throwing around this word, 'risk.'
Risk is likelihood times impact. A threat is who or what could hurt you; a vulnerability is the weakness they'd use; risk is the chance — and the cost — if they do.

beats.title: Three things every control protects — the C-I-A triad
say: Every control in the catalog serves three goals. Remember them as C-I-A.
Confidentiality — keep secrets secret. Integrity — keep data correct and untampered. Availability — keep it working when you need it.
Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. The reason every guardian exists.

beats.title: Three words people mix up
bullets: FISMA (Federal Information Security Modernization Act) — the LAW: U.S. agencies and many contractors must protect their information and systems
NIST SP 800-53 — the CATALOG: the menu of controls you can put to work
The RMF (SP 800-37) — the FRAMEWORK: the step-by-step process that ties it together
say: But why does any of this even exist?
A law says you must protect systems. A catalog lists what you can use. A framework is the process that connects them.
The law is FISMA — the Federal Information Security Modernization Act. 800-53 is the catalog. The RMF is the process. Hold those three apart, and everything else clicks.

beats.plain: Security protects information and systems from unauthorized access, change, or disruption. Privacy protects people from harm caused by how their personal information is handled. They overlap — but they are different goals.
say: Isn't privacy just part of security?
They overlap, but they aim at different things. In Revision 5, security and privacy finally share one catalog.

beats.title: What is NIST SP 800-53?
bullets: A catalog of security AND privacy controls for information systems and organizations
Published by NIST; Revision 5 released in September 2020
The backbone of U.S. federal security — and used widely beyond government too
say: So this 'eight-hundred fifty-three' everyone keeps citing — what even is it?
A catalog. A giant, well-organized menu of controls you put to work.

beats.title: TWENTY FAMILIES, TWENTY DISTRICTS
say: The catalog is organized into twenty families. We'll walk them as twenty districts of the city.
A district isn't really a place — it's a family of related safeguards: policies, processes, and technical controls.
Twenty? That's a lot of ground.
So we'll group them into six layers of defense. By the end, you'll remember every one.

beats.title: Anatomy of a control
bullets: Identifier, e.g., AC-6 (family plus a number)
Discussion, context and rationale, informative, not prescriptive
Related controls, links to other controls
Enhancements, optional add-ons that strengthen it, e.g., AC-6(1)
say: Every control shares the same anatomy. Learn it once, and you can read them all.
First the identifier, like AC-6 — the family and a number.
Then the control itself: the requirement, what you must actually do.
It gives context and rationale — helpful, but not itself a requirement.
Related controls point to neighbors. Enhancements add strength — AC-6(1) is an enhancement of AC-6. References point outside the catalog.
Identifier. Control. Discussion. Related controls. Enhancements. References.

beats.title: Least Privilege
beats.plain: Give every user and process only the access they truly need — nothing more.
beats.why: We'll meet AC-6 again at the Outer Walls. For now, notice the shape: ID, title, meaning.
say: Here's a single control. AC-6, Least Privilege.
Clean. An ID, a title, and what it means.

beats.quote: Employ the principle of least privilege, allowing only authorized accesses for users (or processes acting on behalf of users) that are necessary to accomplish assigned organizational tasks.
say: When you see a gold card, the Archivist is reading the catalog's real words — word for word.
Employ the principle of least privilege, allowing only authorized accesses for users (or processes acting on behalf of users) that are necessary to accomplish assigned organizational tasks.

beats.plain: A ready-made starting set of controls, matched to how much harm a system's failure would cause. You begin from the baseline, then adjust it to fit.
beats.example: Like a 'basic, standard, or premium' security package you start from, then customize.
say: People keep saying 'baseline' — what's that mean?
A starting kit of controls — Low, Moderate, or High, sized to the stakes.

beats.title: BASELINES MOVED OUT
beats.subtitle: Low / Moderate / High now live in NIST SP 800-53B
say: Wait, hold on — where'd the Low, Moderate, and High baselines go?
Sharp eye. In Revision 5 they moved out, into a companion book: 800-53B.
800-53 is the catalog of controls. 800-53B holds the baselines and tailoring guidance.

beats.title: Where 800-53 lives: the Risk Management Framework
say: 800-53 doesn't stand alone. It plugs into a process called the Risk Management Framework.
In plain terms: prepare, decide how much the system matters, pick controls, put them in place, check they work, get a leader to approve it, then watch it forever.
And the catalog is 800-53; the baselines in 800-53B help choose which controls we start with.
We'll walk all seven steps in Act Two. For now, just know the catalog has a home.

beats.title: Your guides
bullets: VEGA — your veteran guide through the Citadel
NOVA — newly appointed; she asks what you're thinking
THE ARCHIVIST — reads the catalog's exact words, on the gold cards
THE NULL — the threats, failures, and bad shortcuts the controls help manage

beats.title: THE ROAD AHEAD — SIX LAYERS
say: Six layers lie ahead. The Outer Walls. The Watchtowers. The Keepers of the Pact.
The High Council. The Forge. The Vaults and Lifelines. Then the finale: Siege Night.

beats.title: Before we ride
bullets: This series is a memory aid, not a substitute for the standard
On-screen IDs and gold quotes are taken from the official catalog
For real decisions, always consult NIST SP 800-53r5 and 800-53B
say: One honest note before we ride out.
The story is ours. The control text is theirs — real, and shown on screen.

beats.q: In plain terms, a security 'control' is best described as:
say: Let's make it stick. What's a control? Pause, and answer.

beats.q: Where do the Low, Moderate, and High control baselines live now?
say: Quick check. Where do the baselines live now? Pause, and answer.

beats.q: How many control families does 800-53 Revision 5 have?
say: And how many families guard our city?

beats.title: Episode 00 — foundations
lines: NIST writes the guidance; an information system is people + tech + data working together.
A control is a safeguard that lowers risk (risk = likelihood × impact); every control serves C-I-A.
FISMA is the law, 800-53 is the catalog, the RMF is the process; baselines live in 800-53B.
beats.mnemonic: Law, catalog, framework — keep them apart.
say: Notebook open. That's the whole map on one page.
That's your foundation. Next, we ride for the Outer Walls.
I'll be waiting at the gate.
